State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Political Assurance .. Administration .. Executive/Legal .. Enforcement .. Statements of Economic Intere
(916) 322-5662       322-5660           322-5901         322-6441         322-6444


June 8, 1982


David Kaplan
General Counsel
SMUD
6201 S Street
Box 15830
Sacramento, CA  95813

Re: Ebasco
    Advice Letter
    No. A-82-108

Dear Dave:

     I am writing in connection with your request for advice
on behalf of SMUD and the Ebasco consultants working for
SMUD.  This letter will confirm our telephone conversation
of June 3, 1982, in which I advised you that the two Ebasco
consultants are required by Government Code Section 87100
to disqualify themselves from certain activities in
connection with the development of proposals for a long
range (20 year) District Resource Plan for SMUD.

FACTS

     The following facts are based on documents provided by
yourself; by SMUD director Rick Castro (with his complaint
filed May 17, 1982); on information provided by yourself,
John Ravera (the SMUD project manager for the long range
plan), and Herb Limmer (Ebasco consultant) at a meeting on
May 21, 1982; on subsequent telephone conversations with
you and Mr. Ravera; and in your letter to me of June 3,
1982.

     SMUD is now in the process of developing a Long Range
(20 year) Resource Plan.  This plan will describe the
energy generation resources, the sources from which
electricity will be purchased and the conservation
(including "load management") measures SMUD intends to use
in the next 20 years to meet its anticipated energy needs.
Adoption of the plan by the SMUD Board will not, by itself,
constitute a legal commitment to use any particular

David Kaplan
June 8, 1982
Page 2


resources; however, it will represent SMUD's intentions as
to future legal commitments, and is likely to be followed
by contract negotiations and actual contracts for those of
the selected resources which are already available, or
which will be available in the near future.

One possible generation resource (there are
approximately 13 now under consideration) is the South
American River Fork ("SOFAR") project. This project is
being developed by the El Dorado Irrigation District, which
is a governmental agency. It is still in the planning
stages, and will go forward only if and when sufficient
purchasers are obtained to make construction economically
feasible. The two major potential customers of SOFAR at
this time are SMUD and P G & E, although some other
utilities are also possible purchasers.

In 1980 the El Dorado Irrigation District entered into
an agreement with Ebasco Services, Inc., for engineering
and project management services for SOFAR. By June 1981,
funds from prospective purchasers had not yet been obtained
and the project was delayed. It was then agreed between
SOFAR and Ebasco, that, to avoid further delay and
resulting cost escalation, Ebasco would proceed with
engineering design services. However, Ebasco would not be
paid for its services until funding was available to SOFAR
from purchasers or from the sale of bonds (as a practical
matter, bonds cannot be sold before purchasers are found).
Ebasco's anticipated fee for its design and project
management services will be approximately $12,400,000. In
addition, when funding is available, Ebasco will receive an
extra incentive payment equivalent to 100% of its
expenditures prior to the availability of funding (this
will be in addition to the reimbursement of these
expenditures). To date, Ebasco has incurred expenses of
over $1,000,000.

If SOFAR is included as one of the generation resources
in the final 20 year plan, contract negotiations between
SMUD and SOFAR (which have already begun on an exploratory
basis) will proceed. A contract would probably follow in
less than a year. Thus, while some parts of the 20 year
plan might not be scheduled for implementation for a number
of years, a SOFAR contract, if included in the plan, would
be concluded within the near future.

David Kaplan
June 8, 1982
Page 3


Proposals for the District Resource Plan are being
developed primarily by SMUD staff. However, the SMUD staff
determined it needed expert consultants for several aspects
of the project. Ebasco was hired to provide these
consultant services.

According to a revised "scope of work," dated April 13,
1982, and incorporated into the SMUD-Ebasco contract,
Ebasco was to provide seven consultants. According to your
June 3, 1982 letter, only three Ebasco employees ever
actually worked on the project. G. Karady, an Ebasco
supervisor, performed some administrative functions at the
Ebasco offices in connection with the project. Two other
Ebasco engineers, Herb Limmer and Lou Rodriguez, have
participated at SMUD headquarters in some aspects of the
project. Limmer and Rodriguez are part of the project task
force operating under Ravera's direction.

The scope of work divides the project into eleven
tasks. According to our conversations and your letter of
April 3, 1982, the services actually being provided by
Ebasco are much more limited than what is described in the
scope of work. A summary of the services Ebasco has
provided, or will provide, under the contract, follows:

Task 1 - Development of the plan scope: Both
Mr. Limmer and Mr. Rodriguez participated in this task,
which is now complete.

Task 2 - Data collection: At our May 21, 1982 meeting,
it appeared that Ebasco's role in this task was limited to
providing generic figures on coal processing plants which
SMUD used to check its figures for the Harry Allan Coal
Project in Nevada.1/ This was done independently by
Ebasco's New York office. (According to your letter,
another consulting firm, R. W. Beck & Associates, was hired
to review data on the Auburn, SOFAR and Calaveras
projects.)

_________________

1/ Your letter states that SMUD ultimately used
Ebasco's figures. However, I was informed by Mr. Ravera
that only SMUD's figures were used in the project.

David Kaplan
June 8, 1982
Page 4


     In your letter, you stated that, in addition to
providing the figures relating to the Havry Allen Project,
Ebasco participated in this task in several ways. First,
Mr. Limmer reviewed and updated figures in a Bechtel study
of a pump storage facility, and assisted in the development
of the load duration curves used to determine the
District's power needs. Second, Mr. Rodriguez reviewed two
of SMUD's existing resources (Rancho Seco and the Upper
American River Project), assisted in establishing criteria
for SMUD to use in determining when and under what
conditions resources can be considered dependable, and
assisted in developing procedures for SMUD to use in
calculating from its load forecasts when it will need
additional resources. Finally, Mr. Rodriguez advised
Mr. Ravera about use of computers in processing the data
collected.

     This task has been completed.

     Task 3 - Load Management and Conservation Options:
Ebasco did not participate in this task, which has been
completed.

     Task 4 - Development of Alternative Plans:
Mr. Rodriguez had responsibility for developing the
"scenarios" for the alternative plans. A scenario is a
description of how a given set of resources, purchases, and
conservation (load management) measures can be pieced
together to provide the energy required under a given 20
year energy load forecast. At our May 21, 1982 meeting, I
was informed that Ebasco had no part in determining which
resources, purchases, conservation options or load forecast
were to be used for each scenario. I understand that Lou
Rodriguez was simply be given a set of options and a
forecast by Mr. Ravera or by Larry Smith (a SMUD supervisor
working on the project), and told to arrange them in a
scenario. In your letter, you add that Mr. Rodriguez
helped determine whether particular combinations of options
would or would not satisfy the projected needs under
specific load management forecasts.

     After each scenario was developed, it was transmitted
to other subgroups of SMUD staff members who analyzed it in
terms of cost, effects on rates, and effects on load
projections.

David Kaplan
June 8, 1982
Page 5


     Mr. Limmer "kept in touch" with this work, "joined in
discussions regarding its progress," and rendered technical
advice.

     This task has been completed.

     Task 5 - Transmission Requirements: Ebasco's
participation in this task was limited. Mr. Rodriguez
assisted SMUD staff in "reworking" data on transmission
requirements so that it could be set up in the same form as
the data collected in Task 2.

     This task has been completed.

     Task 6 - Analysis of Alternatives: I was informed at
our meeting that Ebasco was not participating in this task.

     This task has been completed.

     Task 7 - Preliminary Ranking of Alternatives: At your
suggestion (following our May 21, 1982 meeting), you
advised Mr. Ravera to limit Mr. Limmer's and Mr. Rodriguez'
participation in this task to giving technical advice, and
told them to avoid participation in questions of substance
or policy. Mr. Ravera and Mr. Limmer were present at
meetings on this task over the Memorial Day weekend.

     The ranking in this task is based on six criteria which
have been personally established by Mr. Ravera. According
to your letter, the other participants in the meetings
"merely assisted in making quantitative, technical analyses
to determine which alternatives best fit those criteria."

     This task had been at least partially completed by
June 3.

     Task 8 - Financial Analysis: This task involved
financial analysis of the alternatives chosen in Task 7.
Mr. Limmer will play no role in this task. Mr. Rodriguez
has been requested to limit his participation to answering
technical questions.

     Task 9 - Risk Analysis: Part of this task will be
carried out by use of one Ebasco computer program.
Mr. Limmer and Mr. Rodriguez have been told they may only
offer technical assistance in the use of that program.

David Kaplan
June 8, 1982
Page 6


Ebasco will not participate in any of the other programs or
analyses on this task.

     Task 10 - Final Ranking: This will be done by
Mr. Ravera and by the SMUD general manager, Bill
Walbridge. Ebasco will not participate.

     Task 11 - Final Report: Ebasco's role in this task
will be limited. Mr. Limmer will edit some technical
portions of the report so that the style will be uniform.

                            Analysis

     Government Code Section 871002/ provides:

     No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any
way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to
know he has a financial interest.

     A financial interest is defined by Section 87103:

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

                              * * *

     (c)  Any source of income ... within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made.

     It is clear that Ebasco is a source of income to
Mr. Limmer and Mr. Rodriguez. Section 82030. Therefore,
if they are public officials within the meaning of Section
87100, they are prohibited from making, participating in,
or attempting to influence any governmental decision which
will foreseeably have a material financial effect on
Ebasco. It is equally clear that it is reasonably
foreseeable that a decision by SMUD, either to include or
exclude SOFAR from the 20 year District Resource Plan, will

_____________________________
     2/ All references are to the Government Code unless
otherwise noted.

David Kaplan
June 8, 1982
Page 7


be a major factor in determining whether or not the SOFAR
project will go forward, and, accordingly, whether or not
Ebasco will receive over $13,000,000.  Thus it is
reasonably foreseeable that the SMUD decision on the long
range Resource Plan will have a material financial effect
on Ebasco.  2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b).

     There are, therefore, two questions which must be asked
with respect to Ebasco.  First, are Mr. Limmer and
Mr. Rodriguez "public officials."  Second, are they making,
participating in, or attempting to influence any of the
governmental decisions which may foreseeably have a
material financial effect on Ebasco.

                              1.

     The Political Reform Act specifically includes
consultants within the definition of public official.
Section 82048.  The Commission has, by regulation, further
defined the term consultant.  2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18700(a)(2) provides:

          (2)  "Consultant" shall include any natural
     person who provides, under contract, information,
     advice, recommendation or counsel to a state or
     local government agency, provided, however, that
     "consultant" shall not include a person who:

               (A)  Conducts research and arrives at
          conclusions with respect to his or her
          rendition of information, advice,
          recommendation or counsel independent of the
          control and direction of the agency or of any
          agency official, other than normal contract
          monitoring; and

               (B)  Possesses no authority with respect
          to any agency decision beyond the rendition
          of information, advice, recommendation or
          counsel.

     Mr. Limmer and Mr. Rodriguez both provide, under
contract, information, advice, recommendations or counsel
to SMUD.  Because Mr. Limmer and Mr. Rodriguez essentially
function as additional SMUD staff, under the supervision
and control of the SMUD project manager, they do not come

David Kaplan
June 8, 1982
Page 8


within the exception in 18700(a)(2)(A).  Thus both are
consultants, and, as consultants, are public officials
within the meaning of Section 87100.

2.

In our discussions, we have focused on the questions of
whether the activities of Mr. Limmer and Mr. Rodriguez,
described above, constitute "participation" in the SMUD
decision as to which energy resources to include in the
District Resource Plan.3/  Although when we met on
May 21, 1982, it was my understanding that this project was
still in its early stages, your letter and our recent
telephone conversations make it clear that most of the
staff work had already been completed by June 3.  Because
we can not give advice under Section 82114 as to past
conduct, I have confined my oral advice to you on June 3,
1982, and my advice in this letter, to those parts of the
project which have not yet been completed.

Participating in a governmental decision as defined by
Commission regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(c)
provides:

(c)  A public official or designated employee
"participates in the making of a governmental
decision" when, acting within the authority of his
or her position, he or she:

* * *

(2)  Advises or makes recommendations to
the decision-maker, either directly or
without significant intervening substantive
review, by:

(A)  Conducting research or making
any investigation which requires the
exercise of judgment on the part of the
official or designated employee and the
purpose of which is to influence the
decision; or

_______________________________

3/  As was pointed out, this is the decision which it
is reasonably foreseeable will have a material financial
effect on Ebasco.

David Kaplan
June 8, 1982
Page 9


                    (B) Preparing or presenting any
            report, analysis or opinion, orally or
            in writing, which requires the exercise
            of judgment on the part of the official
            or designated employee and the purpose
            of which is to influence the decision.

     The first issue you raised with respect to this
definition, in a telephone call on May 7, 1982, was whether
or not there was "significant intervening substantive
review" of Mr. Limmer's and Mr. Rodriguez' activities. If
there were significant intervening substantive review,
neither consultant would be participating in any
governmental decisions, and there could be no violation of
Section 87100. I advised you at that time that, without
more facts, I could not say whether or not there was
significant intervening substantive review of the Ebasco
consultants. However, I did point out that the Commission
has interpreted this exception narrowly.

     It is our interpretation that a consultant participates
in a decision, even if it is "reviewed" by several of his
superiors, if those superiors rely on the data or analysis
prepared by the consultant without checking it
independently, if they rely on the professional judgment of
the consultant, or if the consultant in some other way
actually may influence the final decision. Finally, we
pointed out that although Ebasco consultants at most only
participate in recommendations to the SMUD Board, which
makes the final decision, they still may be participating
in the Board's decision. Making recommendations to the
final decision-makers, either directly or indirectly, is
exactly what participating in a governmental decision
means. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(c)(2).

     In your May 10, 1982, memorandum to the SMUD Board you
stated your view that there was "significant intervening
substantive review" of Mr. Limmer's and Mr. Rodriguez'
decisions, but acknowledged that I had advised you that we
interpret the regulation more narrowly.1/

     With respect to the tasks which now remain to be
performed -- tasks 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, it is our view that
there will not be significant intervening substantive

_____________________
1/ Footnote 2 on your May 10, 1982, memo.

David Kaplan
June 8, 1982
Page 10


review of Mr. Limmer or Mr. Rodriguez, as you have
described the situation, if they do in fact offer any
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing, which requires
the exercise of judgment, the effect of which is likely to
influence the final decision. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18702(c)(2)(8).

As we discussed, however, there may be some types of
purely technical assistance which, under the circumstances
of this project, the Ebasco consultants could provide that
would not constitute participating in any governmental
decision which might affect Ebasco.

Actions by public officials which are not intended or
likely to influence a governmental decision (because the
issues or type of discretion or judgment involved in the
actions is unrelated to the outcome of the particular
governmental decision), do not constitute participating in
that decision. For example, a person who decides which
brand of computer to use to develop a SMUD long range plan,
does not participate in the decision as to which energy
generation resources should be included in the plan.
Similarly, there may be technical questions or decisions in
the course of the SMUD project which will not affect the
choice of generation resources in the final plan. For
example, a decision as to what computer programs have the
ability to perform a particular function or type of
analysis is a technical decision which would probably not
foreseeably influence the choice of alternatives in the
final plan. Ebasco consultants could, therefore, advise
SMUD staff on such a technical question without violating
Section 87100, even though they had to exercise
professional judgment in providing the advice.

There may also be types of technical advice which are
"ministerial" in nature, and do not constitute
participating in the governmental decision as to the choice
of energy resources. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18700(d)(1). In this context, an action is ministerial,
even if it requires considerable expertise and professional
skill, if there is no discretion as to the outcome (or at
least, no discretion with respect to any part of the result

_______________________

5/ Such a person might, of course, be making or
participating in a different governmental decision -- the
decision as to what computer SMUD should buy or rent.

David Kaplan
June 8, 1982
Page 11


which could influence the governmental decision in
question).  An example of this would be a complex
calculation for which there is a single "right" answer.
While it might take a person with a great deal of technical
expertise and judgment to find the answer, such a person
would still not be participating in a governmental policy
decision for which the calculation was necessary.6/
However, this exception would not apply to technical tasks,
such as most data gathering or analysis, in which the
consultant makes professional judgments which can affect
the ultimate decision in question.  For example, if there
are several "right" answers to a question, and a consultant
not only does an analysis and calculation, but also decides
or advises as to which is the "best" right answer, his task
is no longer ministerial.  If the choice of which "right"
answer to use will influence the governmental decision, the
consultant is participating in the decision.

     When we first discussed Ebasco's role in the SMUD
project on May 21, 1982, if seemed as if some of
Mr. Limmer's and Mr. Rodriguez' duties might be "technical"
in one of the senses described above, and might not
constitute "participating" in the decision as to which
resources to include in the long range plan.  However,
because the description of Ebasco's role at the May 21
meeting differed from that in the revised scope of work, I
asked you for clarification.  You provided a more detailed
description of their actual role in your June 3, 1982,
letter.  After reviewing the letter, it appeared to be too
late to give advice on most of the tasks where these
exceptions might apply.  Thus, I have only given you advice
as to Tasks 7, 8, 9, 10 and 11.

                            Advice

     Task 7.  Preliminary Ranking of Alternatives.

     The Ebasco consultants cannot participate in this task
(in which SOFAR will be considered as one possible energy
resource) except to the extent that they offer purely
technical advice (which will not foreseeably influence the

_____________________

     6/  This might not be true if the calculations were
so complex that no one else could tell whether it was right
or wrong, however.

David Kaplan
June 8, 1982
Page 12


decision as to the choice of resources or is "ministerial")
concerning any work which they may have done in a prior
task as to which there was no conflict of interests.
Because they may not otherwise participate in the rankings,
they should not be present at the meetings in which the
preliminary rankings are determined.

If SOFAR is finally excluded from all the alternatives
during Task 7, and cannot be reinstated as an option, the
Ebasco consultants may participate in the remaining Tasks.
Otherwise, they may have to disqualify themselves in the
following Tasks.

Tasks 8 and 9. Financial analysis and Risk Analysis.

The Ebasco consultants' role must be limited to giving
the type of permissible technical assistance described
above. From your summary description of their roles in
these Tasks I am unable to determine whether or not their
participation will go beyond the narrow exceptions I
described. However, the use by SMUD of a pre-existing
Ebasco program will not, by itself, create a conflict of
interest.

Task 10. Final Ranking.

Our advice is the same here as for Task 7.

Task 11. Final Report.

Mr. Limmer cannot have any substantive role in writing
or editing the report. Purely technical editing of
technical portions of the report to ensure that they are
comprehensible to a layman and are clear and uniform in
style, if permissible. However, as we discussed, he
should not write or edit portions of the report involving the
final rankings or recommendations.

This advice is essentially the same as the advice I
gave you in our conversation. This letter not only
confirms our prior discussions, but attempts to set forth

David Kaplan
June 8, 1982
Page 13


an analytical framework which I hope you will be able to
apply to other situations in the future. In any event, I
am always happy to discuss these matters with you.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Barbara A. Milman
Chief, Legal Division

BAM:km
cc: Mark Merin

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

May 17, 1982


David S. Kaplan
General Counsel
Sacramento Municipal Utility
District
P.O. Box 15830
Sacramento, CA  95813

Dear Dave:

     Thank you for sending me your May 10, 1982, memo to the
SMUD Board. After reviewing the list of Ebasco's tasks as
described in the memo, I was concerned about SMUD's May 12,
1982, decision to go ahead with the Ebasco Contract. My
concern was increased by our receipt of the enclosed formal
complaint from Rick Castro. On the basis of your memo and
Mr. Castro's complaint, it seems quite likely that in
performing at least some of the contract tasks, Ebasco's
employees will be participating in governmental decisions
within the meaning of the Political Reform Act and the
Commission regulations.

     I think that, for the benefit of everyone involved, it
would be advisable to resolve the conflict of interest
questions now, before any violations of the Political
Reform Act occur. I am certain that you share our desire
to avoid any potential liability under the Act on the part
of Ebasco.

     We will be happy to meet with you, the SMUD project
manager, and a representative of Ebasco to discuss this
matter in more detail. Please let me know when we can set
up such a meeting.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

BAM:plh

B6 The Sacramento Bee • Sunday, April 12, 1987 * *

SOFAR engineering firm plans
to arrange project financing

By Marje Lambert
Bee Staff Writer

PLACERVILLE — Ebasco Services Inc., the New York engineering firm that spent $14 million over seven years on the South Fork of the American River project, now says it also expects to provide last-minute financing for the project.

Robert H. Resch, Ebasco's manager of technical services, Friday told the SOFAR Management Authority that he is "very much encouraged in the progress" toward obtaining the money to build the $690 million water and power project.

"We feel very optimistic ... that we are still on our program, which calls for initiation of construction activities sometime next month," he said.

Ebasco stepped into the dual role as SOFAR's engineer and financier a month ago after a financing arrangement by Catalyst Energy Development Corp. fell apart.

George Hartwell, SOFAR spokesman, emphasized that no agreements have been reached yet. He said Ebasco has not submitted a financing proposal, but the plan is expected to involve a bank loan, with Ebasco buying into the project later

as an equity investor.

The plan is subject to approval later this month by directors of Ebasco Corp., Ebasco's parent firm.

SOFAR directors said they were "cautiously optimistic" that construction will start on the project this spring, in time to meet a Dec. 31, 1990, deadline to begin generating power.

Catalyst, which spent more than two years trying to arrange financing, only to have prospective investors withdraw as the result of a disputed interpretation of tax laws, will not be a participant.

Ebasco expects to proceed regardless of whether it receives the disputed tax benefits, Resch said.

Construction has been delayed for many times for a variety of reasons, making the El Dorado Irrigation District extremely nervous. SOFAR's purpose is to provide the western half of the county with a new water supply. With the rapid growth along the Highway 50 corridor, the irrigation district expects to run out of water for new customers by 1992.

Ebasco had said earlier that construction had to begin last month if the project was to produce power by the end of 1990, a deadline contained in a power-sales agreement with Pacific Gas and Electric Co. Engineers now say they can squeeze the construction schedule and still meet the deadline as long as work begins by June 1.

Ebasco, which has projects all over the world, has been SOFAR's engineer of record since 1980. For the last several years, it has worked on the speculation that SOFAR is not built, the company will not be reimbursed for the estimated $14 million it has invested.

Catalyst, which was brought into the picture by Ebasco in 1984, was given an exclusive agreement to arrange private financing. Had Catalyst succeeded, it would have earned from $5 million to $20 million in development fees plus $1 million or more a year once SOFAR was operating, according to various underwriters analyses.

Once Catalyst's financing scheme fell apart, Ebasco began looking at providing the financing itself. Resch told the Bee earlier that company executives felt it was "sink just the $14 million it had spent, but also "a matter of pride" that SOFAR be built.

Until a financing proposal is submitted, it won't be known if Ebasco would earn development fees.

preliminary rankings are determined. 
If SaFAR is finally excluded from all the alternatives 
during Task 7, and cannot be reinstated as an option, the 
Ebasco consultants may participate in the remaining Tasks. 
Otherwise, they may have to disqualify themselves in the 
following Tasks. 
Tasks 8 and 9. 
Financial analysis and Risk Analysis. 
The Ebasco consultants' role must be limited to giving 
the type of permissible technical assistance described 
above. 
From your summary description of their roles in 
these Tasks I am unable to determine whether or not their 
participation will go beyond the narrow exceptions I 
described. 
However, the use by SMUD of a pre-existing 
Ebasco program will not, by itself, create a conflict of 
interest. 
Task 10. 
Final Ranking. 
Our advice is the same here as for Task 7. 
Task 11. 
Final Report. 
Mr. Limmer cannot have any substantive role in writing 
or editing the report. 
Purely technical editing of 
technical portions of the report to ensure that they are 
comprehensible to a layman and are clear and uniform in 
style, is permissible. 
However, as we discussed, he should 
not write or edit portions of the report involving the 
final rankings or recommendations. 
This advice is essentially the same as the advice I 
gave you in our conversation. 
This letter not only 
confirms our prior discussions, but attempts to set forth 


David Kaplan 
June 8, 1982  
an analytical framework which I hope you will be able to 
apply to other situations in the future. 
In any event, I 
am always happy to discuss these matters with you.

still in its early stages, your letter and our recent 
telephone conversations make it clear that most of the 
staff work had already been completed by June 3. 
Because 
we can not give advice under Section 82114 as to past 
conduct, I have confined my oral advice to you on June 3, 
1982, and my advice in this letter, to those parts of the

sections.facts:
The following facts are based on documents provided by 
yourself; by SMUD director Rick Castro (with his complaint 
filed May 17,1982); on information provided by yourself, 
John Ravera (the SMUD project manager for the long range 
plan), and Herb 1immer (Ebasco consultant) at a meeting on 
May 21, 1982; on subsequent telephone conversations with 
you and Mr. Ravera; and in your letter to me of June 3, 
1982. 
SMUD is now in the process of developing a Long Range 
{20 year) Resource Plan. 
This plan will describe the 
energy generation resources, the sources from which 
electricity will be purchased and the conservation 
(including "load management") measures SMUD intends to use 
in the next 20 years to meet its anticipated energy needs. 
Adoption of the plan by the SMUD Board will not, by itself, 
constitute a legal commitment to use any particular 


David Kaplan 
June 8, 1982  
resources; however, it will represent SMUO's intentions as 
to future legal commitments, and is likely to be followed 
by contract negotiations and actual contracts for those of 
the selected resources which are already available, or 
which will be available in the near future. 
One possible generation resource (there are 
approximately 13 now under consideration) is the South 
Amer ican River For k ("SOFAR n) project. 
T.his project is 
being developed by the El Dorado Irrigation District, which 
is a governmental agency. 
It is still in the planning 
stages, and will go forward only if and when sufficient 
purchasers are obtained to make construction economically 
feasible. 
The two major potential customers of SOFAR at 
this time are SMUD and P G & E, although some other 
utilities are also possible purchasers. 
In 1980 the El Dorado Irrigation District entered into 
an agreement with Ebasco Services, Inc., for engineering 
and project management services for SOFAR. 
By June 1981, 
funds from prospective purchasers had not yet been obtained 
and the project was delayed. 
It was then agreed between 
SOFAR and Ebasco, that, to avoid further delay and 
resulting cost escalation, Ebasco would proceed with 
engineering design services. 
However, Ebasco would not be 
paid for its services until funding was available to SOFAR 
from purchasers or from the sale of bonds (as a practical 
matter, bonds cannot be sold before purchasers are found). 
Ebasco's anticipated fee for its design and project 
management services will be approximately $12,400,000. 
In 
addition, when funding is available, Ebasco will receive an 
extra incentive payment equivalent to 100% of its 
expenditures prior to the availability of funding (this 
will be in addition to the reimbursement of these 
expenditures). 
To date, Ebasco has incurred expenses of 
over $1,000,000. 
If SOFAR is included as one of the generation resources 
in the final 20 year plan, contract negotiations between 
SMUD and SOFAR (which have already begun on an exploratory 
basis) will proceed. 
A contract would probably follow in 
less than a year. 
Thus, while some parts of the 20 year 
plan might not be scheduled for implementation for a number 
of years, a SOFAR contract, if included in the plan, would 
be concluded within the near future. 


David Kaplan 
June 8, 1982  
Proposals for the District Resource Plan are being 
developed primarily by SMUD staff. 
However, the SMUD staff 
determined it needed expert consultants for several aspects 
of the project. 
Ebasco was hired to provide these 
consultant services. 
According to a revised "scope of work," dated April 13, 
1982, and incorporated into the SMUD-Ebasco contract, 
Ebasco was to provide seven consultants. 
According to your 
June 3, 1982 letter, only three Ebasco employees ever 
actually worked on the project. 
G. Karady, an Ebasco 
supervisor, performed some administrative functions at the 
Ebasco offices in connection with the project. 
Two other 
Ebasco engineers, Herb Limmer and Lou Rodriguez, have 
participated at SMUD headquarters in some aspects of the 
project. 
Limmer and Rodriguez are part of the project task 
force operating under Ravera's direction. 
The scope of work divides the project into eleven 
tasks. 
According to our conversations and your letter of 
April 3, 1982, the services actually being provided by 
Ebasco ~re much more limited than what is described in the 
scope of work. 
A summary of the services Ebasco has 
provided, or will provide, under the contract, follows: 
Task 1 - Development of the plan scope: 
Both 
Mr. Limmer and Mr. Rodriguez participated in this task, 
which is now complete. 
Task 2 - Data collection: 
At our May 21, 1982 meeting, 
it appeared that Ebasco's role in this task was limited to 
providing generic figures on coal processing plants which 
SMUD used to check its figures for the Harry Allan Coal 
project in Nevada.ll 
This was done independently by 
Ebasco 's New Yor k off ice. 
(According to your letter, 
another consulting firm, R. W. Beck & Associates, was hired 
to review data on the Auburn, SOFAR and Calaveras 
projects. ) 
II Your letter states that SMUD ultimately used 
Ebasco's figures. 
However, I was informed by Mr. Ravera 
that only SMUD's figures were used in the project. 


David Kaplan 
June 8, 1982  
In your letter, you stated that, in addition to 
providing the figures relating to the Havry Allen Project, 
Ebasco participated in this task in several ways. 
First, 
Mr. Limmer reviewed and updated figures in a Bechtel study 
of a pump storage facility, and assisted in the development 
of the load duration curves used to determine the 
District's power needs. 
Second, Mr. Rodriguez reviewed two 
of SMUD's existing resources (Rancho Seco and the Upper 
American River P'roject), assisted in establishing criteria 
for SMUD to use in determining when and under what 
conditions resources can be considered dependable, and 
assisted in developing procedures for SMUD to use in 
calculating from its load forecasts when it will need 
additional resources. 
Finally, Mr. Rodriguez advised 
Mr. Ravera about use of computers in processing the data 
collected. 
This task has been completed. 
Task 3 -
Load Management and Conservation Options: 
Ebasco did n0t participate in this task, which has been 
completed. 
Task 4 - Development of Alternative Plans: 
Mr. Rodriguez had responsibility for developing the 
"scenarios" for the alternative plans. 
A scenario is a 
description of how a given set of resources, purchases, and 
conservation (load management) measures can be pieced 
together to provide the energy required under a given 20 
year energy load forecast. 
At our May 21, 1982 meeting, I 
was informed that Ebasco had no part in determining which 
resources, purchases, conservation options or load forecast 
were to be used for each scenario. 
I understand that Lou 
Rodriguez was simply be given a set of options and a 
forecast by Mr. Ravera or by Larry Smith (a SMUD supervisor 
working on the project), and told to arrange them in a 
scenario. 
In your letter, you add that Mr. Rodriguez 
helped determine whether particular combinations of options 
would or would not satisfy the projected needs under 
specific load management forecasts. 
After each scenario was developed, it was transmitted 
to other subgroups of SMUD staff members who analyzed it in 
terms of cost, effects on rates, and effects on load 
projections. 


David Kaplan 
June 8, 1982  
Mr. Limmer "kept in touch" with this wor k, "j oined in 
discussions regarding its progress," and rendered technical 
advice. 
This task has been completed. 
Task 5 - Transmission Requirements: 
participation in this task was limited. 
assisted SMUD staff in "reworking" data 
requirements so that it could be set up 
the data collected in Task 2. 
This task has been completed. 
Ebasco's 
Mr. Rodr iguez 
on transmission 
in the same form as 
Task 6 - Analysis of Alternatives: 
I was informed at 
our meeting that Ebasco was not participating in this task. 
This task has been completed. 
Task 7 -
preliminary Ranking of Alternatives: 
At your 
suggestion (following our May 21,1982 meeting), you 
advised Mr. Ravera to limit Mr. Limmer's and Mr. Rodriguez' 
participation in this task to giving technical advice, and 
told them to avoid participation in questions of substance 
or policy. 
Mr. Ravera and Mr. Limmer were present at 
meetings on this task over the Memorial Day weekend. 
The ranking in this task is based on six criteria which 
have been personally established by Mr. Ravera. 
According 
to your letter, the other participants in the meetings 
"merely assisted in making quantitative, technical analyses 
to determine which alternatives best fit those criteria." 
This task had been at least partially completed by 
June 3. 
Task 8 - Financial Analysis: 
This task involved 
financial analysis of the alternatives chosen in Task 7. 
Mr. Limmer will play no role in this task. 
Mr. Rodriguez 
has been requested to limit his participation to answering 
technical questions. 
Task 9 - Risk Analysis: 
Part of this task will be 
carried out by use of one Ebasco computer program. 
Mr. Limmer and Mr. Rodriguez have been told they may only 
offer technical assistance in the use of that program. 


David Kaplan 
June 8, 1982  
Ebasco will not participate in any of the other programs or 
analyses on this task. 
Task 10 - Final Ranking: 
This will be done by 
Mr. Ravera and by the SMUD general manager, Bill 
Walbridge. 
Ebasco will not participate. 
Task 11 - Final Report: 
Ebasco's role in this task 
will be limited. 
Mr. Limmer will edit some technical 
portions of the report so that the style will be uniform.
sections.analysis:
Government Code Section 87100 2/ provides: 
No public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any 
way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
govermental decision in which he knows or has reason to 
know he has a financial interest. 
A financial interest is defined by Section 87103: 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on:
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; CONCLUSION appears before QUESTION

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: ). 
An example of this would be a complex 
calculation for which there is a single "right" answer. 
While it might take a person with a great deal of technical 
expertise and judgment to find the answer, such a person 
would still not be participting in a governmental golicy 
decision for which the calculation was necessary.i7 
However, this exception would not apply to technical tasks, 
such as most data gathering or analysis, in which the 
consultant makes professional judgments which can affect 
the ultimate decision in question. 
For example, if there 
are several "right" answers to a question, and a consultant 
not only does an analysis and calculation, but also decides 
or advises as to which is the "best" right answer, his task 
is no longer ministerial. 
If the choice of which "right" 
answer to use will influence the governmental decision, the 
consultant is participating in the decision. 
When we first discussed Ebasco's role in the SMUD 
project on May 21, 1982, if seemed as if some of 
Mr. Limmer's <and Mr. Rodriguez' duties might be "technical" 
in one of the senses described above, and might not 
constitute "participating" in the decision as to which 
resources to include in the long range plan. 
Howev~r, 
because the description of Ebasco's role at the May 21 
meeting differed from that in the revised scope of work, I 
asked you for clarification. 
You provided a more detailed 
description of their actual role in your June 3, 1982, 
letter. 
After reviewing the letter, it appeared to be too 
late to give advice on most of the tasks where these 
exceptions might apply. 
Thus, I have only given you advice 
as to Tasks 7, 8, 9, 10 and 11. 
Advice 
Task 7. 
Preliminary Ranking of Alternatives. 
The Ebasco consultants cannot participate in this task 
(in which SOFAR will be considered as one possible energy 
resource), except to the extent that they offer purely 
technical advice (which will not foreseeably influence the 
~/ This might not be true if the calculations were 
so complex that no one else could tell whether it was right 
or wrong, however. 


David Kaplan 
June 8, 1982  
decision as to the choice of resources or is "ministerial") 
concerning any work which they may have done in a prior 
task as to which there was no conflict of interest. 
Because they may not otherwise participate in the rankings, 
they should not be present at the meetings in which the 
preliminary rankings are determined. 
If SaFAR is finally excluded from all the alternatives 
during Task 7, and cannot be reinstated as an option, the 
Ebasco consultants may participate in the remaining Tasks. 
Otherwise, they may have to disqualify themselves in the 
following Tasks. 
Tasks 8 and 9. 
Financial analysis and Risk Analysis. 
The Ebasco consultants' role must be limited to giving 
the type of permissible technical assistance described 
above. 
From your summary description of their roles in 
these Tasks I am unable to determine whether or not their 
participation will go beyond the narrow exceptions I 
described. 
However, the use by SMUD of a pre-existing 
Ebasco program will not, by itself, create a conflict of 
interest. 
Task 10. 
Final Ranking. 
Our advice is the same here as for Task 7. 
Task 11. 
Final Report. 
Mr. Limmer cannot have any substantive role in writing 
or editing the report. 
Purely technical editing of 
technical portions of the report to ensure that they are 
comprehensible to a layman and are clear and uniform in 
style, is permissible. 
However, as we discussed, he should 
not write or edit portions of the report involving the 
final rankings or recommendations. 
This advice is essentially the same as the advice I 
gave you in our conversation. 
This letter not only 
confirms our prior discussions, but attempts to set forth 


David Kaplan 
June 8, 1982  
an analytical framework which I hope you will be able to 
apply to other situations in the future. 
In any event, I 
am always happy to discuss these matters with you.

Statements of Economic Intere (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 June 8, 1982 David Kaplan General Counsel SMUD 6201 S Street Box 15830 Sacramento, CA 95813 Re: Ebasco Advice Letter No. A-82-108 Dear Dave: I am writing in connection with your request for advice on behalf of SMUD and the Ebasco consultants working for SMUD. This letter will confirm our telephone conversation of June 3, 1982, in which I advised you that the two Ebasco consultants are required by Government Code Section 87100 to disqualify themselves from certain activities in connection with the development of proposals for a long range (20 year) District Resource Plan for SMUD. FACTS The following facts are based on documents provided by yourself; by SMUD director Rick Castro (with his complaint filed May 17, 1982); on information provided by yourself, John Ravera (the SMUD project manager for the long range plan), and Herb Limmer (Ebasco consultant) at a meeting on May 21, 1982; on subsequent telephone conversations with you and Mr. Ravera; and in your letter to me of June 3, 1982. SMUD is now in the process of developing a Long Range (20 year) Resource Plan. This plan will describe the energy generation resources, the sources from which electricity will be purchased and the conservation (including "load management") measures SMUD intends to use in the next 20 years to meet its anticipated energy needs. Adoption of the plan by the SMUD Board will not, by itself, constitute a legal commitment to use any particular David Kaplan June 8, 1982 Page 2 resources; however, it will represent SMUD's intentions as to future legal commitments, and is likely to be followed by contract negotiations and actual contracts for those of the selected resources which are already available, or which will be available in the near future. One possible generation resource (there are approximately 13 now under consideration) is the South American River Fork ("SOFAR") project. This project is being developed by the El Dorado Irrigation District, which is a governmental agency. It is still in the planning stages, and will go forward only if and when sufficient purchasers are obtained to make construction economically feasible. The two major potential customers of SOFAR at this time are SMUD and P G & E, although some other utilities are also possible purchasers. In 1980 the El Dorado Irrigation District entered into an agreement with Ebasco Services, Inc., for engineering and project management services for SOFAR. By June 1981, funds from prospective purchasers had not yet been obtained and the project was delayed. It was then agreed between SOFAR and Ebasco, that, to avoid further delay and resulting cost escalation, Ebasco would proceed with engineering design services.
